story:
I didn’t want to marry him. I loved him, but the thought of spending forever with that man made me sick. I couldn’t throw up, though—I’d ruin the huge, sparkly, sexy dress I’d paid a fortune for.

Months.

When he proposed, I already knew. I’d found the little silver ring box when I picked up his trousers and tucked the discovery away, pretending surprise for the moment he’d planned. The ring was pretty, almost childish—until he said, “It’s based on a ring you designed when we were in school!” And I remembered. Our literature teacher had assigned us to draw something tied to Romeo and Juliet. We’d argued the day before, so I drew a ring—union—with a bleeding rose, because even beautiful things can hurt. Years later, the rose circled my finger.

I stared at my reflection in a dark wooden mirror and wondered how I’d let it go this far. We were the sweethearts everyone rooted for, together since primary school, the poster couple for happiness and commitment. And there I was, twirling like a sugar-rushed six-year-old, trying not to cry. God bless waterproof mascara.

Months.

The ring wasn’t my only secret. While I was busy making everyone happy with color palettes and party favors, I noticed Carol—my first maid of honor, the sister I chose—wearing a small grey pearl necklace. “That’s cute,” I said. She smiled, touching it, and said nothing. For the first time since we met, I felt her holding something back.

Days later, I drove to her place instead of texting. I saw her car. I saw her in it with someone else. Kissing. Him.

I kept that secret, too. I buried myself in work and seating charts. For sure, he doesn’t love her, I told myself. But do I love him? Enough?

Weeks.

At my bachelorette party, everything was set. All I had to do was enjoy the countdown to the big proof-of-love everyone praised me for. On my way to fix my makeup, I overheard two bridesmaids giggling about Carol’s mystery boyfriend—the one who gave her the pearl. “He’s married,” one said. “He’s got more than one girl,” said the other. “She says it’s a Romeo and Juliet thing.” I drained my champagne and forgot my eyeliner.

That night, I scrolled into a viral clip: a man walking into a church, asking the priest to stop a wedding. Comments argued about whether he loved the groom or was running a scam. Someone posted his name. I knew him. Our parents knew each other. I called my mother for his number.

Hours.

“Ernesto, I need you to wreck my wedding,” I told him. Not to hurt anyone, but to stop the ceremony long enough for the truth to land. We agreed on the fee, the time, the method.

Days.

“Sara? Are you ready?” my father asked through the door.
“Almost,” I said. “Ernesto’s already in that blue suit, stepping over people and apologizing with his hands. He’s nearly at the priest.”
The church was full of family, friends, teachers, strangers. I walked. I held my groom’s hands. He looked at me. Then he looked at her.

“Speak now or forever hold your peace,” said the priest.

“She can’t do it,” someone yelled. Ernesto reached the priest and closed his eyes.

I smiled.

“It’s true. I can’t,” I said. I slipped off the sparkling rose. I whispered to my almost-husband, “Go to your girl with a pearl, and take your rose with you.”

He fell to his knees, sobbing. I turned to the crowd. “He’s been cheating on me with my best friend for over a year. The wedding is over. The party is on.”

I walked out, my bridesmaids trailing—except one. A scuffle started near Ernesto.

Two days later, boarding my honeymoon-turned-girls’ trip, I saw the headline: The wedding wrecker strikes again.
